findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a soft tissue mass situated within Prussak space in the lateral epitympanum. This is accompanied by erosion of the adjacent bony margins, most notably blunting of the scutum and destruction of the middle ear ossicles.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include extension of the soft tissue mass through the aditus ad antrum into the mastoid air cells. There may also be associated opacification and sclerosis of the mastoid air cells indicative of chronic otitis media.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of an expanded facial nerve canal to suggest a facial nerve schwannoma. Furthermore, there is no discrete enhancing mass seated upon the cochlear promontory to suggest a glomus tympanicum, nor is there a dehiscent jugular bulb or aberrant internal carotid artery.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an expansile soft tissue mass in the lateral attic with classic scutum blunting and ossicular erosion.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Acquired cholesteatoma, given the classic location within Prussak's space combined with aggressive local osteoclastic bone erosion. These are epidermoid cysts composed of keratinized squamous epithelium typically resulting from chronic middle ear inflammation and tympanic membrane retraction.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Facial nerve schwannoma, however this lesion typically follows the course of the facial nerve and causes smooth expansion of the bony facial nerve canal. Additionally, a schwannoma would demonstrate contrast enhancement, whereas an uncomplicated cholesteatoma typically does not.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Glomus tympanicum, given that this highly vascular paraganglioma can present similarly as a middle ear mass, and missing it could lead to severe intraoperative hemorrhage if biopsied or surgically excised without preparation. However, a glomus tympanicum characteristically arises on the cochlear promontory rather than in Prussak's space and shows avid contrast enhancement.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to define the full extent of the mass and identify any serious complications such as erosion of the tegmen tympani, labyrinthine fistula, or dehiscence of the facial nerve canal to aid preoperative planning.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is important to monitor the speed of ossicular destruction or mastoid extension, particularly if the patient has a history of chronic otitis media.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the temporal bones including diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), as this modality is superior here for differentiating a cholesteatoma from simple inflammatory granulation tissue or cholesterol granuloma, and is highly sensitive for detecting recurrent disease.
management.mriSequences: I would specify non-EPI diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) as cholesteatomas characteristically demonstrate restricted diffusion, along with T1 post-contrast sequences to distinguish the non-enhancing cholesteatoma from enhancing granulation tissue or a facial nerve schwannoma.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the ENT / Head and Neck Surgery MDT. My report would clearly document the integrity of the ossicular chain, the tegmen tympani, the lateral semicircular canal, and the facial nerve canal to guide definitive surgical excision.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a soft tissue mass situated within Prussak space in the lateral epitympanum. This is accompanied by erosion of the adjacent bony margins, most notably blunting of the scutum and destruction of the middle ear ossicles. Supporting features include extension of the soft tissue mass through the aditus ad antrum into the mastoid air cells. There may also be associated opacification and sclerosis of the mastoid air cells indicative of chronic otitis media. Importantly, there is no evidence of an expanded facial nerve canal to suggest a facial nerve schwannoma. Furthermore, there is no discrete enhancing mass seated upon the cochlear promontory to suggest a glomus tympanicum, nor is there a dehiscent jugular bulb or aberrant internal carotid artery. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an expansile soft tissue mass in the lateral attic with classic scutum blunting and ossicular erosion.

The appearances are most in keeping with Acquired cholesteatoma, given the classic location within Prussak's space combined with aggressive local osteoclastic bone erosion. These are epidermoid cysts composed of keratinized squamous epithelium typically resulting from chronic middle ear inflammation and tympanic membrane retraction. The principle differential would be Facial nerve schwannoma, however this lesion typically follows the course of the facial nerve and causes smooth expansion of the bony facial nerve canal. Additionally, a schwannoma would demonstrate contrast enhancement, whereas an uncomplicated cholesteatoma typically does not. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Glomus tympanicum, given that this highly vascular paraganglioma can present similarly as a middle ear mass, and missing it could lead to severe intraoperative hemorrhage if biopsied or surgically excised without preparation. However, a glomus tympanicum characteristically arises on the cochlear promontory rather than in Prussak's space and shows avid contrast enhancement.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to define the full extent of the mass and identify any serious complications such as erosion of the tegmen tympani, labyrinthine fistula, or dehiscence of the facial nerve canal to aid preoperative planning. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is important to monitor the speed of ossicular destruction or mastoid extension, particularly if the patient has a history of chronic otitis media. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the temporal bones including diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), as this modality is superior here for differentiating a cholesteatoma from simple inflammatory granulation tissue or cholesterol granuloma, and is highly sensitive for detecting recurrent disease. I would specify non-EPI diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) as cholesteatomas characteristically demonstrate restricted diffusion, along with T1 post-contrast sequences to distinguish the non-enhancing cholesteatoma from enhancing granulation tissue or a facial nerve schwannoma. This should go to the ENT / Head and Neck Surgery MDT. My report would clearly document the integrity of the ossicular chain, the tegmen tympani, the lateral semicircular canal, and the facial nerve canal to guide definitive surgical excision.
